definition: Aging populations structurally lower potential GDP growth in advanced economies by approximately 0.5–1.0 percentage points via the Solow labor input channel, as working-age population growth decelerates.
evidence: Working-age population growth decelerated from ~0.8% (1990–2005) to ~0.0–0.2% (2020–2030) in G7
Japan and Germany working-age populations already negative
CBO revised US potential GDP from 2.5% to ~1.8%
European Commission projects EU potential growth falling to 1.1% by 2040
10–15 year demographic projections are among the most reliable forecasts in economics
open_questions: Whether TFP offsets (AI) change the net effect

definition: Dependency ratio inflection points trigger near-mechanical fiscal deterioration under existing entitlement structures, as the worker-to-retiree ratio declines and age-related spending rises as a share of GDP.
evidence: US crossed ~4:1 worker-to-retiree threshold around 2020, heading to ~2.8:1 by 2035
IMF Fiscal Monitor estimates age-related spending rises 3–5% of GDP across advanced economies by 2050
Historical base rate across France, Japan, Italy shows fiscal deficits widen by 2–4% of GDP in two decades following threshold crossing
Social Security trust fund exhaustion projected early-to-mid 2030s
open_questions: Whether this constitutes a crisis for currency issuers or an endogenous sectoral balance adjustment

definition: Immigration policy is the highest-velocity demographic variable for developed-market economies, operating through labor supply, credit creation, consumer demand, and housing formation channels. Current global restrictionist trends represent an underpriced risk.
evidence: CBO estimates net US immigration surged to ~3.3M in 2023, now tightening sharply
Immigration contributes 0.3–0.5pp to potential GDP via labor supply
Immigration drives ~$90–120B in new mortgage origination annually via credit creation
Global restrictionist trend since 2016 is unambiguous across ideological spectrum
Correlated restriction across multiple economies simultaneously has no postwar precedent
open_questions: Whether correlated restriction across multiple economies creates a synchronized demographic shock
Speed and magnitude of policy reversals

definition: Labor scarcity driven by demographic decline erodes credit quality in labor-intensive sectors, particularly healthcare, where the same demographics that create demand (aging patients) drain the labor supply (fewer working-age caregivers).
evidence: Healthcare worker wages up 15–25% since 2020
Skilled nursing and home health operators seeing 200–400bp EBITDA margin compression from labor costs alone
Observable PIK toggle usage and amendment activity as leading indicators
HY index healthcare composition shift from 7% to 12% invalidates historical default model calibrations
open_questions: Whether wage inflation moderates as immigration or automation increases healthcare workforce supply

definition: Aging populations structurally depress the neutral real interest rate (r*) by approximately 100–150 basis points relative to 1980s levels, a finding consistent across both New Keynesian and Post-Keynesian frameworks.
evidence: Laubach-Williams estimates declined from ~3.5% to ~0.5–1.0% pre-COVID
Carvalho, Ferrero & Nechio (2016) estimate demographics alone explain ~1.5pp
Cross-country rank-ordering (Japan < Germany < US < India) is consistent with demographic profiles
Post-COVID bounce to ~1.0–1.5% may partly reflect temporary factors
open_questions: Whether the post-COVID r* increase is temporary or reflects a structural shift
Whether AI productivity gains could raise r* sustainably

definition: China faces a structural demographic reckoning already manifesting economically, with working-age population declining ~5M/year (accelerating to ~8M by 2030), a historically unprecedented TFR of ~1.0 at scale, and related economic deterioration in housing and prices.
evidence: Working-age population declining ~5M/year, accelerating to ~8M by 2030
TFR ~1.0 is historically unprecedented at scale
Housing starts collapsed from ~1.6B to <700M sqm
CPI near 0%, PPI negative for extended periods
open_questions: Whether urbanization buffer, automation, and policy capacity change the speed of decline
Whether TFR 1.0 can be reversed by policy intervention

definition: The stock-bond correlation regime is influenced by demographic structure, with aging-driven labor scarcity acting as a supply shock that biases the correlation toward positive values during stress, materially increasing portfolio volatility for traditional 60/40 allocations.
evidence: Campbell, Sunderam, Viceira (2017) and Ilmanen (2022) show correlation is regime-dependent
Post-2022 episodes of positive correlation are consistent with supply-shock thesis
A 60/40 portfolio assuming −0.2 correlation but realizing +0.3 faces 15–25% higher volatility
Demographic labor scarcity is a supply shock, not a demand shock
open_questions: Whether the correlation regime shift is persistent or episodic
How to hedge the regime transition in portfolio construction

definition: The 2026–2028 maturity wall colliding with demographic-driven labor scarcity creates a dual squeeze on leveraged borrowers from both refinancing costs (higher rates) and operating costs (labor inflation), likely pushing HY defaults above consensus.
evidence: $1.5–3T in maturities depending on scope definition
Refinancing at SOFR+450–600 vs. original SOFR+300–400
Simultaneous 3–5% annual labor cost inflation
Modeled HY default rate reaching 5.2–5.8% vs. consensus ~3.5% in moderate recession
Shrinking peak-spending cohort (35–54) reduces revenue runway for consumer-facing borrowers
open_questions: Timing dependence on recession
How many borrowers have already refinanced

definition: Aging populations create a structural bid for fixed-income assets via pension funds and insurers (~$35–40T globally), compressing credit spreads, but this compression may mask rather than reflect benign credit fundamentals.
evidence: Pension funds and insurers hold ~$35–40T in fixed income globally
ALM-driven demand increases structurally with aging liabilities
Compressed spreads in an aging economy may mask credit deterioration
open_questions: How much demographic spread compression is already priced
Whether the demographic bid provides a floor in downturns or merely delays repricing

definition: Japan provides an instructive but imperfect template for demographic decline in asset markets, with a generalizable sequencing (growth slowdown → rate suppression → currency pressure → fiscal expansion) but structural differences that limit direct extrapolation.
evidence: Japan's 30-year demographic decline provides the longest available analogue
Similarity scoring: Germany ~65%, Korea ~45%, Italy ~40%
Structural differences in reserve currency status, immigration, capital market depth, and policy frameworks limit direct extrapolation
open_questions: Which elements of the Japan sequence are generalizable vs. Japan-specific
Whether per-capita GDP performance (relatively strong in Japan) invalidates the doom narrative

definition: The net effect of demographics on inflation is genuinely ambiguous: the empirical base rate across Japan, Germany, and Korea favors disinflation, but the Goodhart-Pradhan labor scarcity thesis argues for inflation. The uncertainty itself may be tradeable via inflation risk premium.
evidence: Historical base rate: Japan, Germany, Korea all experienced disinflation during demographic decline (3-for-3)
Goodhart-Pradhan thesis: labor scarcity → wage pressure → higher equilibrium inflation
The prior disinflationary era may have been a one-time structural shift (China/Eastern Europe integration)
Uncertainty is a source of inflation risk premium in breakevens
open_questions: Whether services-dominant economies transmit demographic shocks differently than manufacturing-heavy analogues
Whether the post-integration era will produce different inflation outcomes

definition: The retailization of private credit, with $200B+ in semi-liquid vehicles held primarily by 55+ HNW investors offering quarterly liquidity windows against illiquid underlying loans, creates an untested liquidity risk channel with potential for gate-contagion cascades.
evidence: $200B+ in semi-liquid vehicles (interval funds, non-traded BDCs)
Held primarily by 55+ HNW investors
Quarterly liquidity windows against illiquid underlying loans (5–7 year WAL)
No historical stress test exists
Gate-contagion scenario: rational individually, catastrophic in aggregate
open_questions: When and under what macro conditions the aging cohort will draw down
Whether fund gates would trigger broader contagion

definition: ALM-driven pension fund and insurance duration demand concentrates at 20y–30y maturities, creating a structural bid for long bonds while the 7y–10y sector bears the brunt of coupon supply concentration, contributing to persistent belly cheapness in butterfly spreads.
evidence: ALM-driven demand concentrates at specific duration targets as funded status improves
Pension derisking accelerating
Observable in persistent 5s-10s-30s butterfly patterns
7y–10y sector bears the brunt of coupon supply concentration
open_questions: How to isolate demographic effect from liquidity premium and dealer hedging effects

definition: Demographic divergence between growth economies (US via immigration, India via natural growth) and declining economies (China, Germany, Korea, Japan) creates durable but partially priced relative value opportunities across FX, equity, and rates.
evidence: US +0.3–0.5% working-age growth vs. Japan −0.5%, China −0.3%, Korea −0.5%
India +1.0% working-age growth through 2040s
Maps to r* differentials and long-run FX via Balassa-Samuelson
Indian equity already trades at ~22x forward P/E, embedding demographic premium
open_questions: Whether US advantage survives immigration restriction
Whether Indian institutional infrastructure converts demographic potential into output

definition: Equity returns in demographically declining economies are muted but not catastrophic, with demographic drag estimated at 1–2% annualized. The Nikkei experience is the outlier (confounded by bubble burst), not the base case, but starting valuations materially affect outcomes.
evidence: Germany's DAX produced ~6% annualized nominal returns during demographic deceleration at ~12x starting P/E
Demographic contribution to equity underperformance estimated at 1–2% annualized drag
Nikkei conflated demographics with extreme bubble burst
S&P 500 starts at ~20–21x, materially higher than Germany's entry point
open_questions: How the same demographic drag applied to a richly valued US market affects outcomes
Whether buybacks and margin expansion offset demographic revenue drag

definition: Immigration operates as a credit creation channel, not merely a labor supply channel, generating approximately $90–120B in new mortgage origination annually through housing formation and consumption-driven borrowing.
evidence: Estimated $90–120B in new mortgage origination annually from immigration
Operates through housing formation and consumption-driven borrowing
open_questions: Whether credit creation from immigration is a first-order or second-order effect
How immigration restriction affects aggregate credit growth

definition: India's demographic dividend is real in potential but fragile due to education quality deficits, low female labor force participation (~25%), crowded positioning at 20x+ P/E, and the historical base rate showing demographic dividends do not automatically convert to economic output.
evidence: ASER education data shows quality deficits
Female LFPR at ~25%
Indian equity trades at ~22x forward P/E
Historical base rate for demographic dividends materializing is well below 100%
open_questions: Whether institutional infrastructure is improving fast enough
ASER education data and female LFPR trends as key monitoring variables

definition: AI and automation are the primary potential offset to demographic headwinds, but the magnitude and timeline remain highly uncertain, with 2023–2025 productivity data showing only modest improvement despite massive investment.
evidence: McKinsey/Goldman estimate 1–3% annual productivity boost (speculative)
Actual measured US productivity growth 1.5–2.0% in 2023–2025, only modestly above pre-COVID trend
Diffusion lags could mean the effect arrives later
open_questions: Actual measured TFP growth over next 3–5 years
Whether diffusion lags explain the modest improvement so far

definition: The Minsky financial instability cycle may be demographically conditioned, with demographic booms accelerating the hedge→speculative→Ponzi transition and demographic busts creating prolonged balance sheet recessions.
evidence: US (immigration-supported, shorter deleveraging) vs. Japan (no immigration, decades-long balance sheet recession)
Japan private balance swing from −6% to +8% of GDP
open_questions: How to isolate demographic effects from financial deregulation, policy, and other cyclical factors

definition: The fiscal-demographic nexus is a primary driver of term premium, as demographic-driven fiscal costs create persistent supply pressure in government bond markets.
evidence: 2023 repricing as evidence of fiscal supply pressures driving term premium
Large deficits at full employment with rising term premium
open_questions: Whether fiscal costs are monetized or funded by market issuance

definition: The 5y5y forward real rate may encode a demographic signal about long-run neutral rates, with cross-country rank-ordering (US > Eurozone > Japan) consistent with demographic growth differentials, though causation is overstated from correlation.
evidence: Cross-country rank-ordering consistent with demographic growth differentials
Sample is essentially three data points with multiple confounders
open_questions: Whether the rank-ordering reflects demographics or QE intensity
Need for more data points to establish causation

definition: Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia fertility rates may decline faster than UN median projections, driven by smartphone-penetration mechanisms enabling faster diffusion of female education and aspirations, potentially collapsing the 'next billion consumers' thesis.
evidence: Bangladesh TFR fell from 6.6 (1980) to 2.0 (2023) faster than almost any precedent
India crossed below replacement in 2021 ahead of projections
Smartphone-penetration → female education/aspiration diffusion mechanism
open_questions: Whether Sub-Saharan Africa follows South Asian patterns
Investment implications for EM equity, commodities, sovereign debt

definition: The green energy transition may be constrained by shrinking manufacturing workforces in aging economies, creating a tension between demographic decline and ecological transition investment needs.
evidence: Shrinking manufacturing workforces in aging economies
Tension between demographic decline and ecological transition investment needs
open_questions: Whether automation can substitute for demographic workforce shortfalls in green manufacturing

definition: Structurally declining loan origination in aging societies may undermine bank business models as a second-round demographic effect, distinct from borrower-side financial instability dynamics.
evidence: European banking sector weakness is consistent with thesis
Under-theorized even in heterodox literature
open_questions: Whether loan origination declines structurally or is offset by other financial products
Magnitude of the effect relative to other banking headwinds

definition: Pension-adjusted leverage for IG corporates is 0.3–0.5x higher than reported metrics suggest, representing a hidden demographic-linked credit risk.
evidence: Specific quantification of 0.3–0.5x higher leverage when pension obligations included
open_questions: Whether rating agencies adequately capture pension-adjusted leverage
How pension risk transfer activity changes this dynamic

definition: Depopulating areas experience non-linear abandonment cascades where infrastructure, services, and property values decline in self-reinforcing feedback loops.
evidence: Observable in Japan's depopulating regions
open_questions: Whether this pattern generalizes beyond Japan
Threshold population decline rates that trigger cascades
